finding: The round-3 fix for the `tp audit --findings` clean-break gap adds a new checklist item type — `finding` items routed to spec-coverage — but leaves it out of everything the earlier rounds made complete: the unchanged §3.2 ChecklistItem `Type` enum still reads `list_item | table_row | task_acceptance | file_check`, and the §3.2 determinism sentence (rewritten this same round) enumerates id schemes only for table/list/task/file_check items, giving `finding` items no id format at all. This reintroduces the round-1 finding ('item_id scheme shown only by example; format and stability unspecified') that was fixed by making the id scheme exhaustive and deterministic, and it breaks §4.2's requirement that every NDJSON row's item_id 'must match a checklist item id from the prompt'.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision applied to spec/0.23.0.md: finding item type fully wired (§3.2 Type enum + finding-<n> id scheme + Section/SpecLine rules, §3.3 expected_evidence branch); §6.1 batch gate rule deferred to §6.5 skip semantics; ResolveAuditCategory/RenderAuditCategoryText split (§4.4/§18.6/§19.1); §17 shell-import row added; --record/--status parameter-flag rejections (§8.3) and audit flag matrix + explicit audit --status shape (§10.2); gate_timeout write-time rejection rule (§6.7); frontmatter lint object fully shaped (§12.4); security heuristic fallback for files absent at HEAD (§5.2)

suggestion: Add `finding` to the §3.2 Type comment and define its id scheme in the determinism sentence (e.g. `finding-<n>`, `<n>` = zero-based index in the --findings file), stating that ids remain deterministic for a given spec + task file + diff + findings file.

finding: §1.2 routes `finding` items (from `tp audit --findings`) into the spec-coverage checklist, but the ChecklistItem contract never accounts for them: the §3.2 Type comment enumerates only `list_item | table_row | task_acceptance | file_check` (no `finding` value), the §3.2 deterministic-id list covers only `table-<t>-<r>` / `list-<l>-<n>` / `task-<id>` / `file-sec-<n>` / `file-maint-<n>` with no id scheme for finding items, and the §3.3 expected_evidence heuristic has no branch for them while simultaneously claiming 'Every item type yields a non-empty expected_evidence; the field is never null and never empty'. An implementer must invent the type string, the id scheme, and the expected_evidence text (or violate the never-empty guarantee), and also decide what `section` and `spec_line` hold for a finding-derived item (§3.2 defines `spec_line: 0` only for file-level items).

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision applied to spec/0.23.0.md: finding item type fully wired (§3.2 Type enum + finding-<n> id scheme + Section/SpecLine rules, §3.3 expected_evidence branch); §6.1 batch gate rule deferred to §6.5 skip semantics; ResolveAuditCategory/RenderAuditCategoryText split (§4.4/§18.6/§19.1); §17 shell-import row added; --record/--status parameter-flag rejections (§8.3) and audit flag matrix + explicit audit --status shape (§10.2); gate_timeout write-time rejection rule (§6.7); frontmatter lint object fully shaped (§12.4); security heuristic fallback for files absent at HEAD (§5.2)

suggestion: Add `finding` to the §3.2 Type enum, state its id scheme (e.g. the existing `finding-<n>`, zero-based index in the --findings file, noting it in the determinism sentence), define its `section`/`spec_line` values (e.g. section = source findings file path or the finding's location field, spec_line = 0), and add a §3.3 branch such as: for `finding` items, expected_evidence = "verify the fix for: <finding text>" or the finding's recorded resolution evidence.

finding: §6.1 says in batch mode 'the gate runs once iff at least one entry survives the cheap checks, and surviving entries close on gate success'. This contradicts §6.5 item 3 ('When every entry carries a non-empty skip_gate, the gate is not executed at all') for a batch whose surviving entries all carry skip_gate, and contradicts §6.4 ('entries carrying skip_gate close with the skip recorded' even when the gate fails): surviving skip entries close regardless of gate outcome, not 'on gate success'. A tester reading §6.1 writes a test asserting the gate command executes and gates all closes; a tester reading §6.5/§6.4 (and TestDoneGate_AllSkipBatchNoRun in §19.2) asserts the opposite.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision applied to spec/0.23.0.md: finding item type fully wired (§3.2 Type enum + finding-<n> id scheme + Section/SpecLine rules, §3.3 expected_evidence branch); §6.1 batch gate rule deferred to §6.5 skip semantics; ResolveAuditCategory/RenderAuditCategoryText split (§4.4/§18.6/§19.1); §17 shell-import row added; --record/--status parameter-flag rejections (§8.3) and audit flag matrix + explicit audit --status shape (§10.2); gate_timeout write-time rejection rule (§6.7); frontmatter lint object fully shaped (§12.4); security heuristic fallback for files absent at HEAD (§5.2)

suggestion: Rewrite the §6.1 batch clause: the gate runs once iff at least one surviving entry does NOT carry a non-empty skip_gate; surviving non-skip entries close on gate success; surviving skip entries close regardless of gate outcome.

finding: §1.2 routes `finding` items (one per entry of a `tp audit --findings` file) into the spec-coverage checklist, but §3.2's ChecklistItem.Type comment enumerates only `list_item | table_row | task_acceptance | file_check`, the deterministic-id rule names id schemes only for table-/list-/task-/file- items, and §3.3's expected_evidence rules cover only task_acceptance, table_row/list_item, and file-level items — while §3.3 simultaneously claims 'Every item type yields a non-empty expected_evidence'. The `finding` type has no declared Type value, no id scheme, and no expected_evidence rule, contradicting the determinism and never-empty claims.

suggestion: Add `finding` to the Type enum in §3.2, name its id scheme (e.g. the existing `finding-<n>` scheme) in the deterministic-id paragraph, and add an expected_evidence rule for `finding` items in §3.3 (e.g. the keyword-search fallback text).

finding: The round-3 sentence 'Every item type yields a non-empty expected_evidence; the field is never null and never empty' (which replaced the unreachable 'Empty fallback: ""' branch flagged in round 2) is falsified by the same round's addition of `finding` items in §1.2: the §3.3 heuristic enumerates branches only for task_acceptance, table_row/list_item, and file-level items, so a `finding` item has no defined expected_evidence — the universal claim the fix established is undone by the new item type it doesn't cover.

suggestion: Add a fourth branch to §3.3 for `finding` items (e.g. "verify resolution of finding: <finding text or resolution evidence excerpt>") so the 'every item type' guarantee holds.

finding: The new §6.1 sentence 'the gate runs once iff at least one entry survives the cheap checks, and surviving entries close on gate success' re-opens the all-skip_gate ambiguity fixed in round 1: when every batch entry carries a non-empty skip_gate and passes cheap checks, the 'iff' mandates a gate run while §6.5 item 3 mandates 'the gate is not executed at all'. It also contradicts unchanged §6.4, under which surviving entries carrying skip_gate close with the skip recorded even when the gate fails — not 'on gate success'.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision applied to spec/0.23.0.md: finding item type fully wired (§3.2 Type enum + finding-<n> id scheme + Section/SpecLine rules, §3.3 expected_evidence branch); §6.1 batch gate rule deferred to §6.5 skip semantics; ResolveAuditCategory/RenderAuditCategoryText split (§4.4/§18.6/§19.1); §17 shell-import row added; --record/--status parameter-flag rejections (§8.3) and audit flag matrix + explicit audit --status shape (§10.2); gate_timeout write-time rejection rule (§6.7); frontmatter lint object fully shaped (§12.4); security heuristic fallback for files absent at HEAD (§5.2)

suggestion: Reword §6.1 to defer to §6.5: 'the gate runs once iff at least one surviving entry does not carry skip_gate; surviving entries without skip_gate close on gate success, and skip_gate entries close per §6.4/§6.5 regardless of the gate result.'

finding: gate_timeout_seconds is 'settable' via tp set --workflow with range 30-3600, but the out-of-range rule is defined only for values 'in a task file' (fallback to 600 plus validate warning). Whether 'tp set --workflow gate_timeout_seconds=10' is rejected at write time (as the range-validated convergence fields suggest) or accepted with the read-time fallback applying is unspecified — two testers would assert different exit codes for the same command.

suggestion: State the write-time rule explicitly, e.g. 'tp set --workflow rejects out-of-range gate_timeout_seconds with exit 1; the 600 fallback applies only to hand-edited task files'.

finding: 'Mirrors §8.5 for audit rounds' leaves the exact tp audit --status output shape underdetermined: §8.5's shape uses the key review_rounds and includes mechanical_checks, but audit --check 'does not run workflow.checks'. Whether the audit key is audit_rounds or review_rounds, and whether mechanical_checks appears (registered list, empty, or omitted), cannot be determined from the spec, so a golden-output test cannot be written.

suggestion: Spell out the audit --status shape explicitly, e.g. '{audit_rounds, consecutive_clean, required_clean_rounds, converged, stale}' with mechanical_checks omitted.

finding: The frontmatter lint object {present, lines, domain, lens_roles} is underdetermined: lens_roles is never defined (all keys present under tp.lens, or only keys with a non-empty list — does 'architect: []' from the §12.1 example appear? does 'all' count as a role?), and the values of lines/domain/lens_roles when present is false (null, omitted, or defaults such as 'software') are unspecified. Two testers writing tp lint --json golden tests would produce different expected objects.

suggestion: Define lens_roles (e.g. 'lens keys with a non-empty question list, in schema order, including all') and give the exact no-frontmatter shape (e.g. {present: false, lines: null, domain: 'software', lens_roles: []}).

finding: Mode-vs-parameter-flag combinations are unspecified: §8.3 item 6 defines exclusivity of --record/--status only against other modes, so behavior of --record/--status combined with --round, --findings, --diff-from, or --spec-inline (exit 2, ignored, or honored) is undefined; §10 states no flag rules at all for tp audit, so --record vs --status exclusivity and combinations with --affected-files/--findings are undefined. A tester cannot pick the expected exit code for e.g. 'tp review spec.md --record f.ndjson --round 3'.

suggestion: State for each mode which non-mode flags are accepted, ignored, or rejected (e.g. '--record/--status reject --round/--findings/--diff-from/--spec-inline with exit 2'), and add an audit flag-interaction rule mirroring §8.3 item 6.

finding: The security content heuristic reads a file's 'first 200 lines at the HEAD revision', but when --affected-files replaces the diff universe the list may contain paths that do not exist at HEAD (new or untracked files — the spec itself anticipates non-diff files via the +0/-0 annotation fallback). Whether such a file's content heuristic falls back to the working tree, is skipped (path heuristic only), or causes the file to be dropped is unspecified, so security-role membership for that file is untestable.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision applied to spec/0.23.0.md: finding item type fully wired (§3.2 Type enum + finding-<n> id scheme + Section/SpecLine rules, §3.3 expected_evidence branch); §6.1 batch gate rule deferred to §6.5 skip semantics; ResolveAuditCategory/RenderAuditCategoryText split (§4.4/§18.6/§19.1); §17 shell-import row added; --record/--status parameter-flag rejections (§8.3) and audit flag matrix + explicit audit --status shape (§10.2); gate_timeout write-time rejection rule (§6.7); frontmatter lint object fully shaped (§12.4); security heuristic fallback for files absent at HEAD (§5.2)

suggestion: Define the fallback, e.g. 'for files absent at HEAD the content heuristic reads the working-tree file; files unreadable in both places are evaluated by the path heuristic only'.

finding: §6.1 states that in batch mode 'the gate runs once iff at least one entry survives the cheap checks', but §6.5 item 3 (and test TestDoneGate_AllSkipBatchNoRun in §19.2) requires the gate NOT to run when every entry carries a non-empty `skip_gate` — such entries pass all cheap checks (closure verification still applies to them), so the 'iff' is literally contradicted; the mixed case where the only entries surviving cheap checks all carry `skip_gate` is likewise left with two conflicting answers.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision applied to spec/0.23.0.md: finding item type fully wired (§3.2 Type enum + finding-<n> id scheme + Section/SpecLine rules, §3.3 expected_evidence branch); §6.1 batch gate rule deferred to §6.5 skip semantics; ResolveAuditCategory/RenderAuditCategoryText split (§4.4/§18.6/§19.1); §17 shell-import row added; --record/--status parameter-flag rejections (§8.3) and audit flag matrix + explicit audit --status shape (§10.2); gate_timeout write-time rejection rule (§6.7); frontmatter lint object fully shaped (§12.4); security heuristic fallback for files absent at HEAD (§5.2)

suggestion: Restate §6.1's batch rule as: the gate runs once iff at least one entry that survives the cheap checks does not carry a non-empty `skip_gate`.

finding: ResolveAuditCategory is assigned two incompatible behaviors: §4.4 and §18 step 6 say it 'renders' the §2.2 category enum and precedence text embedded in every audit prompt's Output Schema block, while §19.1 (TestAuditCategory_PrecedenceResolution) specifies it as a resolver mapping a list of applicable categories to a single category ('[correctness, security] → security'). One function cannot both return a category value and render the schema text block.

suggestion: Split the responsibilities into two named functions (e.g. ResolveAuditCategory for precedence resolution, RenderAuditCategoryText for the Output Schema block) and update §4.4 and §18 step 6 to reference the rendering function.

finding: The round-3 rule that plain `tp import` overwrites an existing zero-task init shell without `--force` is a v0.22.0-to-v0.23.0 behavior change (v0.22.0 required `--force` to overwrite any existing task file), but no row was added to §17's compatibility tables — reintroducing the omission pattern round 1 fixed when §13's validate/coverage changes were added to §17.
